High strength-to-weight alloys for aerospace and medical.

Aluminum matrix composite reinforced with SiC particles.

en.name: ≈ indicates approximate analog by composition/strength; weldability/impact toughness can differ.
Mechanical properties depend strongly on heat treatment; bearing steel ШХ15 ≈ 100Cr6.
Match by minimum tensile strength and elongation; graphite form differs (flake vs spheroidal).
Stabilized grades (Ti/Nb) affect weldability; corrosion resistance varies with Mo/N content.
Temper (T, T6, T651) matters; marine alloys 5083/АМг6 require correct Mg/Mn levels.
Conductivity depends on oxygen/impurity content; OFHC analogs match low O content.
Lead content affects machinability; corrosion behavior depends on Zn.
Select analogs by tin/Al/Ni/Fe content and bearing properties.
Heat treatment and interstitials (O, N) alter strength and ductility; match by alpha/alpha‑beta type.
Sort by visual/defect grading; mechanical values vary with moisture.
Strength grading differs between standards; use structural class mapping.
Bonding type and veneer species drive durability/water resistance.
Melt index and density classes define processing; UHMWPE analogs by molecular weight.
Random copolymers for pipes; thermal stability & pressure rating per standard.
Stabilizers and plasticizers affect temperature/UV resistance.
Match by optical clarity, impact grade, and thickness spec.
Water absorption affects properties; consider filled grades.
Glass transition (Tg) and hardener type determine performance; follow cure schedules.
Use corrosion/heat‑resistant iso grades for marine.
Isocyanate/polyol types control UV stability and hardness; moisture sensitive.
Crosslink system (perox/S) affects temp & chemical resistance.
Acrylonitrile content controls oil/fuel resistance & low‑temp.
Pay attention to peroxide vs platinum cure for biocompatibility.
Match by dielectric strength/porosity for electrical vs tableware uses.
Use same purity and grain size to match wear/strength.
Stabilizer type governs toughness and aging behavior.
Mechanical match depends on fiber volume fraction and orientation.
Compare modulus/strength per fiber and resin Tg; prepreg cure cycles matter.
CTE and thermal conductivity drive equivalence for electronics/rotors.
Match by chemistry and temperature capability; heat treatment windows differ.
Strength after aging depends on Ni‑Ti‑Mo chemistry; welding requires precautions.
Austenite finish (Af) temperature defines application window.
